retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Tuberculosis -- Pathophysiology -- Oral Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: TB involving the oral cavity is rare. The mandible, buccal mucosa, lips, gingiva, and tongue can be involved with lesions that appear as painless or painful ulcers, nodules, fissures, and granulomas. The lesions can be of acute or chronic onset. Biopsy, acid-fast staining, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), and culture are required to establish the diagnosis. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Tuberculosis -- History and Physical -- Orificial tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: Orificial TB is a rare form of CTB that presents as a painful ulcer that does not heal. Due to self-inoculation in those with pulmonary TB or gastrointestinal involvement, this form is more commonly found in the oral cavity of immunosuppressed individuals. It can also occur in the nares, vulva, and peri-anal mucosa (see Image . Orificial Tuberculosis). Miliary tuberculosis

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculosis is a granulomatous disease caused by aerobic acid-fast bacilli, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, triggering a primary pulmonary infection. Oral tuberculosis results from a secondary infection via blood dissemination. [4] The spread of M. tuberculosis occurs through aerosols generated by coughing, sneezing, or speaking. The bacteria can remain airborne within tiny droplets for hours and infect susceptible individuals. The risk of tuberculosis transmission in a dental practice appears low, however, possible, primarily through patients from high-risk areas of the world or with reactivated tuberculosis infection. [2] Scarlet Fever

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Oral tuberculosis lesions are more commonly located in the posterior aspect of the dorsum of the tongue, followed by the buccal mucosa, gingiva, lips, and the floor of the mouth. [14] Primary oral tuberculosis manifests as a single painful, necrotic ulcer that can expand from the sulcular epithelium and the epithelium of the oral cavity to the base of the bordering vestibule. [1] [17] The ulcer can last more than 2 to 3 weeks. Primary lesions are also associated with the spread of the disease to cervical lymph nodes that present as enlarged and painful. Tuberculosis osteomyelitis may also appear as lesions within the jaw. Secondary tuberculosis presents as slowly growing, irregular, and painful oral ulcers that exhibit a thick white mucus at the base and do not self-resolve. Like primary lesions, secondary lesions are usually exposed to mechanical trauma. [4] [14] [23] Some other systemic symptoms that may accompany a patient with tuberculosis are fever, chronic cough, sputum production, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, and appetite loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If tuberculosis is suspected, different tests are available, including tissue histology, tuberculin skin test, acid-fast bacillus microbial staining, and culture or polymerase chain reaction. A biopsy of any unresolved ulcer is mandatory to confirm the diagnosis of tuberculosis and rule out carcinoma. If the tuberculin skin test returns positive, a confirming chest X-ray is required because patients who received the BCG vaccine (tuberculosis vaccine) can have a false positive tuberculin skin test. [14] Scarlet Fever

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy commonly shows caseating granulomas, typically with central necrosis surrounded by epithelioid histiocytic cells, Langerhans giant cells, and lymphocytic infiltration, characteristic of tuberculosis. [14] [15] Scarlet fever There are no specific histological changes in scarlet fever; however, a neutrophilic infiltrate with spongiosis and parakeratosis in the epidermis may be seen. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial lesions of the oral mucosa can appear throughout the oral cavity and often serve as the earliest indication of an underlying condition. In some cases, these diseases present without symptoms during the initial phase, creating significant diagnostic challenges. This activity provides a comprehensive overview of the most common bacterial lesions, including syphilis, tuberculosis, gonorrhea, and scarlet fever. It emphasizes the importance of early recognition of oral manifestations to ensure timely and accurate diagnosis. By focusing on the clinical presentation, diagnostic process, and treatment strategies, the activity highlights both the potential complications of missed or delayed recognition and the critical role of prevention in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol is used to treat tuberculosis. These drugs are given daily for the initial 2 months and then another 4 months of just isoniazid, rifampin, and ethambutol. A chest physician must provide and supervise this therapy to ensure adherence and reduce the chances of drug resistance. Patients should also be isolated during their treatment course to prevent the further spread of disease. Some second-line agents include injectable drugs (aminoglycosides, amikacin) and fluoroquinolones. [14] The BCG vaccine against tuberculosis is provided in developing countries to prevent the spread of the disease. Scarlet Fever

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Blastomycosis: Blastomycosis lesions resemble oral carcinoma both clinically and histopathologically. Hence, careful histological demonstration of yeasts is essential. [29] The clinical course may also mimic bacterial infections like syphilis, tuberculosis actinomycosis, or autoimmune diseases like Crohn's disease and systemic lupus erythematosus. Cryptococcosis: Clinically, oral cryptococcosis appears similar to histoplasmosis, paracoccidioidomycosis, tuberculosis, and malignancy. [59] Paracoccidioidomycosis: These oral lesions mimic malignancies and other granulomatous lesions. [30] Geotrichosis: Oral geotrichosis resembles oral candidiasis. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Tuberculosis -- Introduction -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: True cutaneous TB Exogenous cutaneous tuberculosis (inoculation TB) Tuberculous chancre [No prior TB exposure] Tuberculosis verrucosa cutis [Previous TB immunity] Lupus vulgaris [Previous TB immunity] Post-BCG vaccination Subcutaneous abscess Regional lymphadenopathy Ulceration Keloid Lupus vulgaris Scrofuloderma Endogenous cutaneous tuberculosis (originating from a primary site of TB) Contiguity or autoinoculation [Low TB immunity] Scrofuloderma: arising from contiguous focus (eg, lymph node, bone, joint, epididymis) Tuberculous orificialis: arising from autoinoculation of mucocutaneous tissues (eg, oral, nasal, anogenital) from a primary TB focus Lupus vulgaris Hematogenous dissemination (low TB immunity) Lupus vulgaris Tuberculous gumma (ie, abscess) Acute miliary TB Tuberculid (hypersensitivity id like reaction to Mtb antigens) Papulonecrotic tuberculid Lichen scrofulosorum Erythema induratum of Bazin [1] [6] [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculosis is more common in developing countries. In 2019, more than 80% of tuberculosis cases occurred in Indonesia, India, China, the Philippines, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nigeria, and South Africa. Most oral manifestations of tuberculosis are secondary to the primary pulmonary infection; however, in younger patients, primary oral tuberculosis may occur from direct inoculation of the organism in the oral mucosa. [10] Around 1% to 5% of patients have oral manifestations, and 1.33% of those cases are associated with immunocompromising infections such as HIV. [10] [11] Scarlet fever can occur in all age groups, but it is more prevalent amongst children between 5 and 15 years old. Strep throat is responsible for 15% to 30% of all pharyngitis in children and 5% to 15% in adults, making the prevalence of scarlet fever higher in children. Scarlet fever is more prevalent in underdeveloped countries due to crowded living conditions. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Immunocompromised patients, such as those with HIV, have weaker immune systems, making it harder for the body to control the tubercular bacteria. Drug-resistant bacteria can spread infections throughout the body and cause further complications, including spinal pain, meningitis, and miliary tuberculosis. [27] Scarlet Fever

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the etiology of syphilis, gonorrhea, tuberculosis, and scarlet fever. Differentiate signs, symptoms, patient history, and sexual and travel history. Evaluate the diagnostic tests to diagnose syphilis, gonorrhea, tuberculosis, and scarlet fever. Describe the treatment of syphilis, gonorrhea, tuberculosis, and scarlet fever. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Histoplasmosis: Chronic histoplasmosis primarily affects the lungs, causing clinical manifestations similar to tuberculosis, including fever, weight loss, cough, dyspnea, and chest pain. Oral histoplasmosis is clinically indistinguishable from malignancy. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Cutaneous Fistula -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Because orocutaneous fistulas (OCF) are uncommon, misdiagnosis of those from a dental origin is not unusual. In the study of Lee et al., the majority of patients were misdiagnosed initially. Oral cutaneous fistulas were thought to be an epidermal cyst (24,2%), furuncle (21.2%), subcutaneous mycosis (15.2%), squamous cell carcinoma (9.1%), basal cell carcinoma (6.1%), and foreign body granuloma (6.1%). [4] The differential diagnosis include tuberculosis infection, pyogenic granuloma, suppurative lymphadenitis, salivary gland fistula, and carcinoma. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Tuberculosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the prognosis of CTB is generally better than in individuals with internal organ involvement or immunocompromised individuals. However, even with aggressive treatment, the outcome may be poor in immunocompromised patients or those harboring drug-resistant organisms. Among the different forms of CTB, mortality rates are higher in tuberculous gumma (approximately 66%) and miliary CTB, with mortality rates reaching up to 75%. [6] Additionally, scarring is a common complication of healed CTB lesions. If untreated, lupus vulgaris can eventually undergo malignant transformation to squamous carcinoma. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both anxiety (القلق) and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Anxiety can manifest as vague chest discomfort, making it an important psychosomatic contributor to this symptom. Oral candidiasis, a fungal infection of the mouth, may also be linked to nonspecific chest pain, potentially through systemic effects, medication side‑effects, or underlying immunosuppression that predisposes patients to both conditions. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may signal either anxiety or oral thrush—or a combination of both—helps clinicians pursue a more comprehensive evaluation and tailor management to address the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1726
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) is characterized by fatigue (التَّعَب) and may present with nonspecific chest pain. Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is also clinically associated with both nonspecific chest pain and fatigue. The overlap of fatigue and nonspecific chest pain in anxiety and oral candidiasis highlights the need for careful clinical assessment to differentiate between psychological and infectious etiologies, ensuring appropriate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that these agents are used for local oral therapy and belong to the broader category of dental and oral care products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 482
retrieved_docs.content: mineralization, serving a therapeutic role in skeletal remodeling. Beklomethason is indicated for generic inflammation (الالْتِهاب), and aspiration pneumonitis is mapped to respiratory inflammation, illustrating overlapping inflammatory pathways across organ systems. Generic inflammation is directly related to respiratory inflammation, and other bone diseases and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with both generic inflammation and bone resorption, reflecting the interplay between inflammation and skeletal degradation. Oral diseases (excluding dental conditions) are organized under salivary‑gland dysfunction, reflecting anatomical connections. Blood coagulation factors are contraindicated with tranexamic acid, indicating a therapeutic restriction to avoid adverse coagulation effects. Other central nervous system infections and poliomyelitis are subcategories of bulbar inflammation, emphasizing their involvement of the brainstem bulbar region. Finally, other connective tissue

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2575
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck displays morphological characteristics that are associated with السرطان النقيلي, indicating that the histopathologic appearance of the metastatic tumor in cervical lymph nodes reflects the typical morphology of السرطان النقيلي and aids in accurate diagnosis and appropriate management of patients with head‑and‑neck metastatic disease.
